text: let yourself be cast into the flames!” Everyone was acutely aware of the Yevesektzia spies present at the farbrengen, listening to every word spoken. Duchman writes, “Everyone was in dread.” Althaus recalls, “We gazed at the agents, their faces flushed with anger. We knew that it was only a matter of time before something terrible would happen.” Rabbi Eliezer Poupko[[162: Jem Oral History Project.]] was the rabbi in the Russian town of Velizh. A local Communist activist confided to him that the Rebbe would be arrested shortly. He decided to travel to St. Petersburg to warn the Rebbe. The Rebbe was unfazed by Rabbi Popko’s report, addressing him in deferential third-party Yiddish, “Velizh Rav, I am ready to go on mesirat nefesh [to give up my life].” As Rabbi Popko told his grandson Mordechai Dovber, “The Rebbe would not budge.”
